subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Elected Legislature vs. Independent Judiciary in Preserving Limited Government
stimulus: There is continued debate over how to best preserve the democratic ideal of limited government. Develop an argument as to whether an elected legislature or an independent judiciary is more effective in preserving limited government.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Federalist No. 51, Federalist No. 78, Article I of the Constitution of the United States.)
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row A — Thesis/Claim): Responds to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis establishing a line of reasoning about whether an elected legislature or an independent judiciary is more effective in preserving limited government.

Examples that earn the point: 'An elected legislature is more effective at preserving limited government because they are elected by the majority and are held accountable to the will of the people.' OR 'An elected legislature is better able to preserve limited government because checks and balances allow the legislature to limit the other branches of government.' OR 'An independent judiciary is better able to preserve limited government because checks and balances allow the judiciary to limit the other branches of government.' OR 'The independent judiciary is more effective at preserving limited government because they are not accountable to the wishes of the majority.'
parts.scoring_notes: Must take a clear position. Generic statements about limited government being debated do not earn the point. Saying 'the executive branch best protects limited government' does not earn the point (not responsive to the prompt).

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
rubric_criteria: 0 pts: No accurate evidence, or evidence not relevant to the topic.
1 pt (Row B — 1 point): One piece of evidence relevant to the topic.
2 pts (Row B — 2 points): One piece of specific and relevant evidence supports the claim OR two pieces relevant to the topic.
3 pts (Row B — 3 points): Two pieces of specific and relevant evidence, one from a listed document (Federalist 51, Federalist 78, or Article I) (requires Row A).

Examples of acceptable evidence: 'Federalist No. 51 argues that ambition must counteract ambition and that separation of powers creates the checks necessary for limited government.' 'Federalist No. 78 argues that an independent judiciary with lifetime tenure can stand against majority tyranny and protect the Constitution.' 'Article I of the Constitution gives Congress the power to pass legislation and override vetoes, providing a legislative check on executive and judicial power.' 'Judicial review allows courts to strike down unconstitutional laws, preserving limited government.' 'Elected legislators are accountable to voters who can remove them for overstepping government power.' 'Marbury v. Madison established judicial review as a key mechanism for limiting government power.'
parts.scoring_notes: To earn 3 points, must have earned Row A, and one piece of evidence must come from Federalist 51, Federalist 78, or Article I.

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row C — Reasoning): Uses reasoning to explain how evidence supports an argument relevant to the prompt.

Examples that earn the point: 'Federalist 78 argues that an independent judiciary is necessary to protect the Constitution from temporary majority passions — because judges are not elected and have lifetime tenure, they can make unpopular decisions that uphold limited government without fear of political retaliation.' 'Article I gives Congress enumerated powers and the ability to check the executive, which means the legislature directly embeds limits on government power into the constitutional structure — making it more effective than a judiciary that can only react to violations of limits already established.'
parts.scoring_notes: Must have provided at least one piece of specific and relevant evidence. Must explain the logical connection between evidence and the thesis.

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row D — Alternate Perspective): Responds to an opposing perspective using rebuttal or refutation. Must describe an alternate perspective AND rebut or refute it.

Examples that earn the point: 'While an elected legislature is better at reflecting the people's will, legislators are subject to political pressure and may be reluctant to limit popular but unconstitutional measures — the judiciary's independence from electoral politics makes it better suited to preserve limits on government.' 'Although the judiciary can strike down unconstitutional laws, an unelected judiciary that overrides democratic majorities may itself become a form of limited government — elected legislators who are directly accountable to voters are better positioned to define the limits the people want.'
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND rebut or refute it — not merely acknowledge it.
